insights: High-cost expense reports (top tertile) take a median of 24 days to process—6× longer than low-cost reports (4 days)—indicating a severe and systematic cost-driven processing delay that directly undermines throughput equity across expense values.

Processing time increases monotonically with expense amount: low-, medium-, and high-cost brackets show median processing times of 4, 10, and 24 days respectively, confirming that cost is not merely associated with delay but functions as the dominant structural driver of processing duration.

The extreme positive correlation between expense amount and processing time (Pearson r = 0.994, R² = 0.989) indicates that processing time is almost entirely determined by cost, leaving little room for other operational or contextual factors to meaningfully modulate turnaround.

This cost–time relationship holds uniformly across all seven departments—including Customer Support, IT, and Sales—with no department showing attenuation or reversal, confirming the delay is systemic rather than localized to specific teams or governance practices.

Within expense categories that support valid bracket comparisons (e.g., Travel and Miscellaneous), high-cost reports still incur dramatically longer processing times (23.5 vs. 5 days in Travel; 19 vs. 3 days in Miscellaneous), ruling out category composition as an explanatory confounder.

Two major categories—Services (0 high-cost reports) and Assets (only 1 low-cost report)—exhibit severe cost-bracket imbalance, suggesting current policy or submission behavior systematically excludes high-value expenses from Services or concentrates them exclusively in Assets, introducing structural bias in category-level analysis.
summary: The analysis conclusively demonstrates that expense amount is the overwhelming determinant of processing time—nearly perfectly predictive (R² = 0.989)—with high-cost reports taking six times longer to process than low-cost ones. This monotonic, department-agnostic, and category-robust pattern reveals a systemic design or policy feature that prioritizes scrutiny or approval rigor for higher-value expenses, but at the direct cost of equitable throughput: lower-value reports are processed rapidly while high-value ones face prolonged delays regardless of department or category. Such a structure risks creating bottlenecks in financial close cycles, distorting cost-center accountability timelines, and disadvantaging employees submitting smaller, routine expenses. The next priority should be auditing whether this delay reflects intentional multi-tier review logic—and if so, evaluating its necessity—or whether it stems from unaddressed automation gaps, manual handoffs, or inconsistent escalation protocols in high-cost workflows.
